edges.description: A neurovascular flare state can produce abnormal distal perfusion patterns.
Neurovascular dysfunction drives recurrent painful attacks in distal extremities.
Abnormal shunting contributes to red, hot, painful distal attacks.
Sensory neuron hyperexcitability feeds into the final painful flare phenotype.

nodes.description: Aspirin can be effective particularly in secondary erythromelalgia related to myeloproliferative disease.

evidence.snippet: The most useful oral medications for erythromelalgia seem to be aspirin

nodes.description: The final common clinical expression is recurrent burning pain with erythema in the distal extremities, exacerbated by heat and alleviated by cooling.

evidence.reference_title: Erythromelalgia. Part I: Pathogenesis, clinical features, evaluation, and complications.
evidence.snippet: It is characterized by episodes of extremity erythema and pain that can be disabling

nodes.description: One long-standing pathogenic model proposes that abnormal microvascular arteriovenous shunting contributes to the hot, erythematous, painful flare phenotype.

evidence.snippet: A wide variety of etiological conditions can cause erythromelalgia, all having a single common pathogenetic mechanism - microvascular arteriovenous shunting.

nodes.description: Management is individualized and focuses on pain reduction, flare prevention, and avoidance of unsafe cooling behaviors.

evidence.reference_title: Erythromelalgia. Part II: Differential diagnoses and management.
evidence.snippet: The goal of management is pain control, reduction of flare frequency, and prevention of complications.

nodes.description: Erythromelalgia is best understood as a heterogeneous neurovascular pain syndrome rather than a single-gene disorder, with altered sensory signaling and vascular regulation contributing to flares.

evidence.reference_title: Erythromelalgia. Part I: Pathogenesis, clinical features, evaluation, and complications.
evidence.snippet: Erythromelalgia is a rare pain disorder that is underrecognized and difficult-to-treat.

nodes.description: Severe burning extremity pain is the core symptomatic burden of erythromelalgia.

evidence.reference_title: Erythromelalgia. Part I: Pathogenesis, clinical features, evaluation, and complications.
evidence.snippet: Erythromelalgia is a rare pain disorder

meta.association: Gain-of-function causal variant in a minority of primary cases

evidence.reference_title: Genetic Variants in the SCN9A Gene are Detected in a Minority of Erythromelalgia Patients.
evidence.snippet: Overall, in this cohort only 4/34 (11.7%) of unrelated patients had erythromelalgia likely caused by gain-of-function variants in SCN9A.

nodes.description: A subset of primary erythromelalgia cases is caused or modified by gain-of-function SCN9A variants that increase Nav1.7-dependent nociceptor excitability.

evidence.reference_title: Genetic Variants in the SCN9A Gene are Detected in a Minority of Erythromelalgia Patients.
evidence.snippet: Gain-of-function variants in the voltage-gated sodium channel Nav1.7, encoded by the SCN9A gene, have previously been identified in patients with erythromelalgia
Primary erythromelalgia is nowadays considered to be a genetically determined neuropathic disorder affecting SCN9A, SCN10A, and SCN11A coding for NaV1.7, NaV1.8, and NaV1.9 neuronal sodium channels.
